title: What is a Logic Gate?
concept: A logic gate is the simplest possible decision rule: it takes one or more true/false inputs and outputs exactly one true/false answer.

exercises.prompt: Recap: When you built the text classifier, what turned raw words into something the model could actually use?

options.text: Numeric features extracted from the text, like word counts
The model read the sentences the way a human would
Nothing — the words went in exactly as written

exercises.explain: Right! Every model needs its inputs turned into something it can decide on. Today we rewind all the way to the simplest decision-maker of all: the logic gate.

cards.title: The simplest question in the world
cards.body: Long before smart apps, long before computers as we know them today, there was one tiny question: is this true, or is this false? Answering that question, over and over, is where digital decision-making secretly began.

cards.title: What is a logic gate?
cards.body: A logic gate is the simplest possible decision rule. It takes one or more inputs that are each either true or false, applies one fixed rule, and produces exactly one output: true or false.

cards.title: A gate that only lets certain things through
cards.body: Think of a gate at a fairground. A guard checks a rule — "are you tall enough?" — and either lets you through or turns you away. A logic gate does the exact same thing, just with true/false answers instead of people.

cards.title: Three basic gates
cards.body: AND: true only if every input is true ("has a ticket AND is on the list"). OR: true if at least one input is true ("has a ticket OR is on the list"). NOT: flips a single input ("NOT raining" turns raining = true into raining = false).

cards.title: Not just inside computer chips
cards.body: You might think logic gates only exist as tiny electronic parts inside a computer chip. In reality, the logic is everywhere — a thermostat, a car alarm, even a house rule ("you can watch TV AND finish your homework") is a logic gate in disguise.

cards.title: AND vs. OR at a glance
cards.body: The one-word difference that changes everything:

left.text:
Needs EVERY input true
Stricter, harder to satisfy

right.text:
Needs at least ONE input true
Looser, easier to satisfy

exercises.prompt: A door alarm should trigger if the door is open AND it is nighttime. What kind of gate is this?
exercises.explain: Correct! Both conditions — door open, and it's nighttime — must be true for the alarm to trigger. That's the strict AND rule.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An OR gate needs every single input to be true before it outputs true.
exercises.explain: False. An OR gate only needs at least one input to be true — that's what makes it the "looser" gate.

exercises.sentence: A ___ gate flips a single true/false input into its opposite.
exercises.explain: A NOT gate takes one input and flips it: true becomes false, and false becomes true.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is an example of a NOT gate in real life?

options.text: A porch light that turns on only when it is NOT daytime
A car that starts only with a key AND a buckled seatbelt
A door that opens with a ticket OR a guest pass

exercises.explain: Right! Flipping "is daytime" into "turn on the light" is exactly what a NOT gate does — it inverts one condition.

exercises.prompt: A car will only start if the key is in the ignition AND the driver's seatbelt is buckled. Which logic gate models this rule, and why?

options.text: AND, because both conditions must be true for the car to start
OR, because only one condition needs to be true
NOT, because it flips a condition
None — cars don't use logic like this

exercises.explain: Exactly! Both the key AND the seatbelt must be true, so the car only starts on that one strict combination. Next: what happens when you start wiring gates together to build bigger, multi-step decisions?
